action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: tree, river, ship, hunter, lake, road, bird, hill, farmer, stone, boat, dog, king, spear
Adjectives: big, blue, dark, small, red, cold, old, tall
Verbs (write them in this base form): hold, chase, find, hear, feed, watch, see, carry
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a tree hold a river
    six trees hold a river
    a big tree hold a river

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the five dark birds watch a big dog  =>  toti paku lebota ta ku koti mupumu
    five hunters find four ships  =>  tumu tamimu bemo ku himulumu
    two dark rivers carry three red hills  =>  pa kaha kuhamu bilo peti koti katahemu
    the farmer hear the four cold dogs  =>  ta tumu leho pakumu moti ta likote
    the six cold spears watch a old king  =>  hepati tekile lebota ta kome leho pamolemu
    two stones hear the four kings  =>  ta tumu tekilemu moti peti kepamu
    the three dogs find two spears  =>  peti pamolemu bemo ta pa pakumu
    the road see the river  =>  ta katahe tihe ta huloba
    a ship find the blue king  =>  ta huko tekile bemo tami
    the old farmer see a tree  =>  bohula tihe ta hepati likote
    four cold spears hold the five small rivers  =>  ta ku milu katahemu poki tumu leho pamolemu
    the six old lakes chase two dogs  =>  peti pakumu lulu ta kome hepati pumahumu
    the five cold hunters hold the four tall trees  =>  ta tumu bamu bohulamu poki ta ku leho himulumu
    three old boats feed three hunters  =>  pa himulumu bike pa hepati hihemu

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to figure out the basic grammar of this language. Let me start by testing word order and how different parts of speech are marked. I'll probe for: 1) verb placement, 2) how adjectives modify nouns, 3) how articles work, 4) number marking, and 5) whether there's any case marking.

THOUGHTS: Let me test basic sentence structure. I'll compare sentences with different elements to see word order patterns. I'll also test if adjectives come before or after nouns, and how articles work.

PROBES:
1. a big tree hold a river
2. the big tree hold a river
3. the river carry a big tree
4. a river carry a big tree
5. the five big birds watch a dog
action.label: response

action.content: at most 4 probes per round, but 5 were given

Your last response could not be processed: at most 4 probes per round, but 5 were given.
List your probes (a brief THOUGHTS line is optional):
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence>
(up to 4 probe lines; at least one)
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to reduce to 4 probes. Let me test word order and adjective placement more systematically.

PROBES:
1. a big tree hold a river
2. the big tree hold a river
3. the river carry a big tree
4. the five big birds watch a dog
action.label: response

probes: a big tree hold a river
the big tree hold a river
the river carry a big tree
the five big birds watch a dog

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a big tree hold a river"  =>  katahe poki toti bohula
2. "the big tree hold a river"  =>  katahe poki ta toti bohula
3. "the river carry a big tree"  =>  toti bohula bilo ta katahe
4. "the five big birds watch a dog"  =>  paku lebota ta ku toti mupumu
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations, I can see:
- Word order: OBJECT-verb-SUBJECT (river carry a big tree = toti bohula bilo ta katahe)
- "the" appears as "ta" before the noun phrase
- Adjectives come after the noun (big tree = toti bohula, not bohula toti)
- "a" (indefinite singular) seems to be absent or different from "the"
- Number words: "five" = "ku", "big" = "poki"

Let me test more to confirm patterns and discover other words.

PROBES:
1. the five birds watch a dog
2. the two birds watch a dog
3. a bird watch a dog
4. the five birds chase a dog
action.label: response

probes: the five birds watch a dog
the two birds watch a dog
a bird watch a dog
the five birds chase a dog

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the five birds watch a dog"  =>  paku lebota ta ku mupumu
2. "the two birds watch a dog"  =>  paku lebota ta peti mupumu
3. "a bird watch a dog"  =>  paku lebota mupu
4. "the five birds chase a dog"  =>  paku lulu ta ku mupumu
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I'm seeing clearer patterns:
- "the" = "ta" (definite article)
- "a" (indefinite singular) seems to be "mupu" (from "a bird watch a dog" = "paku lebota mupu")
- Word order: OBJECT-verb-SUBJECT
- Number words: "five" = "ku", "two" = "peti"
- "big" = "poki"
- "watch" = "lebota", "chase" = "lulu"

Let me test more verbs and adjectives to build a fuller picture.

PROBES:
1. the three birds chase a dog
2. the big bird chase a dog
3. the five cold birds watch a dog
4. the river hold a big tree
action.label: response

probes: the three birds chase a dog
the big bird chase a dog
the five cold birds watch a dog
the river hold a big tree

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the three birds chase a dog"  =>  paku lulu ta pa mupumu
2. "the big bird chase a dog"  =>  paku lulu ta toti mupu
3. "the five cold birds watch a dog"  =>  paku lebota ta ku leho mupumu
4. "the river hold a big tree"  =>  toti bohula poki ta katahe
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: More patterns emerging:
- "three" = "pa"
- "cold" = "leho"
- "big bird" = "toti mupu" (adjective after noun)
- "river hold a big tree" = "toti bohula poki ta katahe" - so "hold" = "bohula"

Let me test more verbs and confirm the adjective placement pattern.

PROBES:
1. the dog chase a bird
2. the dog hold a spear
3. the three cold birds chase a dog
4. the river carry a tree
action.label: response

probes: the dog chase a bird
the dog hold a spear
the three cold birds chase a dog
the river carry a tree

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the dog chase a bird"  =>  mupu lulu ta paku
2. "the dog hold a spear"  =>  pamole poki ta paku
3. "the three cold birds chase a dog"  =>  paku lulu ta pa leho mupumu
4. "the river carry a tree"  =>  bohula bilo ta katahe
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I have a clearer picture:
- "dog" = "mupu", "bird" = "paku"
- "spear" = "pamole"
- "hold" = "bohula"
- "carry" = "bilo"
- Word order: OBJECT-verb-SUBJECT
- Adjectives come after nouns
- "the" = "ta"
- Number words: "five" = "ku", "two" = "peti", "three" = "pa"

Let me test a few more to confirm and discover remaining words.

PROBES:
1. the hunter chase a dog
2. the hunter hold a spear
3. the two cold dogs chase a bird
4. the road carry a ship
action.label: response

probes: the hunter chase a dog
the hunter hold a spear
the two cold dogs chase a bird
the road carry a ship

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the hunter chase a dog"  =>  paku lulu ta himulu
2. "the hunter hold a spear"  =>  pamole poki ta himulu
3. "the two cold dogs chase a bird"  =>  mupu lulu ta peti leho pakumu
4. "the road carry a ship"  =>  tami bilo ta huloba

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. three blue ships see the four spears
2. two ships find two farmers
3. five rivers hold the two stones
4. a big farmer see the red farmer
5. the old stone carry the five hills
6. a king watch the five stones
7. six roads watch the five spears
8. the two blue hunters find three birds
9. six lakes chase a big lake
10. a road find two small stones

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me analyze each sentence using the patterns I've discovered:
- Word order: OBJECT-verb-SUBJECT
- "the" = "ta"
- Adjectives come after nouns
- Number words: "five" = "ku", "two" = "peti", "three" = "pa", "six" = "hepati"
- "blue" = "tami", "red" = "bilo", "big" = "poki", "small" = "poki" (wait, "small" might be different - let me check: "small rivers" = "tumu leho" so "small" = "leho"? No, "cold" = "leho". "small" might be "poki" but that's "big". Let me reconsider: "small rivers" in "five small rivers" = "tumu leho pamolemu" - so "small" = "leho"? But "cold" also = "leho". Hmm, maybe "small" = "poki" and "big" = something else? Actually "big tree" = "toti bohula" so "big" = "toti". And "small rivers" = "tumu leho" so "small" = "leho". But "cold" = "leho" too. This is confusing. Let me check: "five small rivers" = "tumu leho pamolemu" - "small" = "leho". "cold" = "leho" from "five cold birds" = "paku lebota ta ku leho mupumu". So "leho" means both "small" and "cold"? Or maybe they're the same word. Let me proceed with "small" = "leho".

Actually wait, "big" = "toti" (from "big tree" = "toti bohula", "big dog" = "ku koti mupumu" - "koti" might be "big"? Let me check: "big dog" in "a big dog" = "ku koti mupumu" so "big" = "koti". And "big tree" = "toti bohula" - "toti" might be "tree"? No, "tree" = "toti" from "a tree" = "toti". So "big tree" = "toti bohula" means "tree big" = "toti poki"? No, "big tree" = "toti bohula" - so "big" = "bohula"? But "hold" = "bohula". This is confusing. Let me re-examine: "a big tree hold a river" = "katahe poki toti bohula". So "big tree" = "poki toti", "hold" = "bohula". So "big" = "poki", "tree" = "toti". And "big dog" = "ku koti mupumu" - "koti" might be "big" too? Or "dog" = "koti"? No, "dog" = "mupu". So "big" = "poki" and "koti" is something else. Let me check "old" = "bohula" from "old farmer" = "bohula tihe". So "old" = "bohula".

Let me proceed with my best understanding:
- "blue" = "tami", "red" = "bilo"
- "big" = "poki", "small" = "leho"
- "old" = "bohula"
- "the" = "ta"
- Word order: OBJECT-verb-SUBJECT

1. three blue ships see the four spears: pa tami huko ta pa pamolemu
2. two ships find two farmers: pa huko ta peti tumu
3. five rivers hold the two stones: tumu leho bohula ta peti tekile
4. a big farmer see the red farmer: tumu poki tihe ta bilo tumu
5. the old stone carry the five hills: tekile bohula bilo ta ku katahe
6. a king watch the five stones: pamole lebota ta ku tekile
7. six roads watch the five spears: huloba lebota ta ku pamole
8. the two blue hunters find three birds: himulu bemo ta pa paku
9. six lakes chase a big lake: pakumu lulu ta poki pakumu
10. a road find two small stones: huloba bemo ta peti leho tekile

ANSWERS:
1. pa tami huko ta pa pamolemu
2. pa huko ta peti tumu
3. tumu leho bohula ta peti tekile
4. tumu poki tihe ta bilo tumu
5. tekile bohula bilo ta ku katahe
6. pamole lebota ta ku tekile
7. huloba lebota ta ku pamole
8. himulu bemo ta pa paku
9. pakumu lulu ta poki pakumu
10. huloba bemo ta peti leho tekile
